title: 2FA Advice
content:
Overview


Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) is often the source of customer confusion and frustration. Agents should be prepared to guide customers through common 2FA issues with patience and clarity. Most problems stem from lost devices, app changes, or misunderstood recovery codes.



Common Scenarios & How to Handle Them


"I got a new phone and can't log in":


"No worries — this happens all the time. If you still have your old phone, open your authenticator app on that device and use the code to log in. Once you're in, disable 2FA from your settings, then re-enable it on your new phone. If you don't have the old phone, we'll need to verify your identity and disable 2FA from our side. Then you can set it up fresh on your new device."


"I lost my recovery codes":


"Recovery codes are single-use backups for situations exactly like this. If you've lost them and can't access your authenticator app, we'll need to verify your identity and disable 2FA so you can re-enroll and generate new codes. Going forward, I recommend saving them in a password manager so they're always accessible."


"My authenticator app isn't working":


"Sometimes the app's time can drift out of sync. Try going into your authenticator app's settings and selecting 'Sync time' or 'Time correction for codes.' If that doesn't work, delete the Tklesson entry from the app and we'll re-enroll you. But first, let's verify your identity."


"I'm locked out after too many attempts":


"I can see your account has been locked due to multiple failed 2FA attempts. This is a security feature. Let's verify your identity now and I'll get you back in."


"Do I really need 2FA?":


"2FA is one of the best ways to protect your account. Without it, someone who gets your password could access everything — your sessions, your credits, your personal information. It takes less than two minutes to set up and gives you peace of mind. I highly recommend keeping it enabled."



Recommending Authenticator Apps


  • Google Authenticator — Simple, free, no backups. Best for users who want minimal setup.
  • Authy — Cloud backups, multi-device sync. Best for users who change phones often or want protection against device loss. Recommended for most users.
  • Microsoft Authenticator — Good for users already in the Microsoft ecosystem.


Preventive Advice to Give Customers


  • "Save your recovery codes somewhere safe — a password manager, a locked note, or even a printed copy."
  • "Don't store recovery codes on the same phone you use for authentication. If you lose the phone, you lose both."
  • "If you're switching phones, disable 2FA on the old phone first, then re-enable on the new one. Or use Authy — it backs up your codes to the cloud."
  • "Tklesson will never ask for your 2FA code. If anyone does, it's a scam."


When to Escalate


  • Customer cannot pass identity verification but insists they are the account owner.
  • Customer claims their account was compromised and 2FA was enabled without their knowledge.
  • Multiple 2FA disable requests from the same account in a short period — possible fraud indicator.
